text: In this video, we will demonstrate a robotic-assisted lingula sparing left upper lobectomy with segmental plane identification using near-infrared fluorescence imaging. The patient is an eighty-six-year-old male with a past medical history of emphysema and extensive smoking history, as well as diabetes and hypertension. The CT scan identifies a two-centimeter left upper lobe nodule central in position in the apical anterior-posterior division. This was shown preoperatively by CT biopsy to be an adenocarcinoma. His preoperative workup identified an FEV-I of forty-three percent of predicted and a DLCO of seventy-eight percent of predicted. Perfusion to the left lung on VUQ scan was twenty-eight point six percent and decision was made to pursue segmentectomy and possible lobectomy.

The surgeon employs a three-arm robotic approach, utilizing a grasping retractor in the furthest right assistant robotic port, a monopolar cautery in the right working arm, and a fenestrated bipolar grasper in the left working arm. The initial dissection involves opening the superior anterior hilum and identifying the level X and level V AP window lymph nodes, which are then dissected free.

The surgeon uses a monopolar cautery dissector to perform fine dissection around the apical anterior divisions of the vein, the pulmonary artery, and the first truncus branch of the pulmonary artery on the left side.
The fourth assistant robotic arm gently retracts the lung anteriorly, allowing the surgeon to approach the posterior hilum. The posterior pleura is then opened, revealing the underlying structures.

The surgeon performs initial dissection in the posterior hilum, targeting the removal of level ten lymph nodes. The inferior vein is identified, and level seven lymph nodes are excised and sent for frozen section analysis, returning as benign. The dissection continues to approach the interlobar pulmonary artery.

The surgeon uses a spatula cautery for fine dissection, identifying the posterior hilar interlobar pulmonary artery, which marks the posterior boundary of the major fissure. The anterior fissure is identified, and the fissure between the lingula and the lower lobe is divided. The lung is retracted anteriorly, and additional dissection reveals the lingular vein, distinct from the apical anterior and posterior segmental veins.

The surgeon circumferentially dissects the anterior apical posterior segmental veins using robotic instruments. A robotic stapler with a vascular load is then applied under direct control to divide and ligate these veins.

The surgeon identifies and circumferentially dissects the first truncus or apical anterior branch of the upper lobe pulmonary artery. A robotic stapler is used to pass a vascular load posterior to the artery under direct control.

Following the division of the truncus artery, the surgeon performs additional hilar dissection to open the mediastinal pleura, revealing a posterior branch of the pulmonary artery. The posterior apical and anterior bronchus are identified and freed. The posterior branch of the pulmonary artery is then ligated using a robotic stapler under direct control.

The surgeon divides the bronchus under direct control, isolating the vascular supply of the apical anterior and posterior divisions. Endocyanin green is infused intravenously, highlighting the intersegmental plane with perfusion visible only to the lingula.

The surgeon uses pinpoint electrocautery to mark the intersegmental plane, highlighting the contrast between perfused and non-perfused areas of the lung. The robotic stapler is then used under direct control to divide the intersegmental plane, ensuring resection of non-perfused lung tissue.

The surgeon uses near-infrared fluorescence imaging to confirm resection of all non-perfused lung areas while sparing the lingula. The lung is gently manipulated under direct control for precise stapler handling. The resected specimen is placed in a retrieval bag and removed through an access incision.

The completed resection of the lung is visible, showing the area where the tumor was located within the apical anterior and upper division. The resected specimen is identified as an invasive adenocarcinoma, papillary dominant histological type, measuring 2.3 centimeters, with negative margins.
